title: Bringing Pets to the Netherlands
metaTitle: Bringing Pets to the Netherlands: Rules, Documents & Travel Tips
subtitle: EU import rules, documents, and timing for relocating with a dog or cat.
description: Moving to the Netherlands with a dog or cat? Learn about pet import rules, microchip and rabies requirements, documents, airline travel, housing, costs, and what to do after arrival.

image.alt: Expat with dog and luggage by a canal in the Netherlands; suitcase and pet carrier visible.

quickAnswers.label: Summary
quickAnswers.value: Dogs and cats can usually enter the Netherlands if they meet EU pet travel rules: identification, rabies vaccination, and valid veterinary paperwork in the right order before departure.

quickAnswers.label: Who this is for
quickAnswers.value: Expats moving to the Netherlands with a dog or cat—from another EU country or from outside the EU.

quickAnswers.label: Key requirement / risk
quickAnswers.value: Timing: microchip, rabies vaccination, and waiting periods must match official rules for your route. Late planning often delays travel, not missing a single form.

quickAnswers.label: Typical timing
quickAnswers.value: Start planning weeks to months before travel, depending on origin country and whether your pet has never been vaccinated or documented in the EU system.

sections.heading: Import rules, identification, and documents
body: Pets entering the Netherlands usually need to meet EU non-commercial movement rules. In practice that means: correct identification (microchip), an up-to-date rabies vaccination in the right sequence, and health documentation issued within valid windows.

Requirements differ by country of origin (EU vs non-EU, risk status), species, route, and airline. An EU pet passport is common for pets already in the EU system; pets from outside the EU often need a different health certificate before an EU passport applies. Always confirm current rules with official sources and your vet before booking.
bullets: Microchip (usually before or with rabies vaccination, per current rules)
Rabies vaccination and any required waiting period before travel
Health certificate or EU pet passport, as applicable to your route
Airline booking confirmation for cabin, hold, or cargo—rules vary by carrier and pet size

callout.title: Documents and timing go together
callout.text: Some certificates are only valid for a limited period before travel. Plan the sequence (chip → vaccination → waiting period → travel date) with your vet, not as a last-minute task.

links.label: Documents needed to move to the Netherlands

sections.heading: Airline travel and indicative costs
body: International pet travel depends on airline rules, approved crate sizes, cabin vs hold vs cargo, and sometimes seasonal temperature limits. Not every route treats pets the same way—confirm with the carrier before you book.
Indicative ranges below are for planning only; your vet, airline, and any relocation company set actual prices.
bullets: Cabin vs hold vs cargo depends on pet size, airline, and route
Use an IATA-compliant crate when required
Check transit and connection rules for multi-leg flights

callout.title: Plan early to control cost
callout.text: Pet relocation is usually cheapest when planned early. Reissued documents or flight changes add up quickly.

links.label: Cost of moving to the Netherlands

sections.heading: Finding pet-friendly housing
body: Not every rental in the Netherlands allows pets. In tight markets, pet-friendly listings can take longer—start your housing search alongside your travel planning, not after you arrive.
bullets: Confirm pet rules in writing before you sign a lease
Allow extra time in competitive cities

links.label: Register your address in the Netherlands

sections.heading: After arrival: checks, vet, and settling in
body: After a long trip, most pets need a calm routine and a local vet for registration or a post-travel check. Entry procedures depend on how you travelled—follow any instructions from the carrier or border process that applied to your route.
Give your pet time to adjust: familiar bedding, steady feeding times, and gradual exploration of the new home and neighbourhood.
bullets: Keep vaccination and identification paperwork accessible after arrival
Register with a local veterinarian when needed
Rebuild walking and feeding routines quickly
Watch appetite and behaviour for a few days after travel

services.name: AniCura Netherlands
services.description: Veterinary clinic network in the Netherlands for post-arrival checks and routine care.
services.indicativeCost: e.g. €50–€150 for check-up; fees vary by clinic.

services.name: Zooplus Netherlands
services.description: Online pet supplies for crates, food, bedding, and settling-in essentials.

sections.heading: Planning checklist (before travel and after arrival)
body: Use this as a planning aid alongside your wider move timeline—not a replacement for official advice or a full relocation checklist.
steps: Confirm import rules for your country of origin and travel route (official sources + vet).
Verify microchip and rabies vaccination sequence and any waiting period.
Prepare veterinary documents within the correct validity windows.
Research airline pet policies before booking flights
Buy an approved crate if your pet will travel in hold or cargo
Confirm pet-friendly housing rules before signing a lease
Plan first-week veterinary contact and supplies after arrival
Add pet tasks to your moving checklist so dates and paperwork stay aligned

callout.title: Common mistake
callout.text: Treating pet travel as a last-week task. Most issues come from timing and sequencing, not from a single missing checkbox.

howToSteps.text: Ensure vaccination and any waiting period align with rules.

howToSteps.text: Confirm cabin, hold, or cargo options before booking.

howToSteps.name: Confirm pet-friendly housing
howToSteps.text: Agree pet rules in writing before signing a lease.

howToSteps.name: Plan arrival vet and supplies
howToSteps.text: Line up local vet contact and essentials for the first week.

faq.q: Can I bring my dog or cat to the Netherlands?
faq.a: Yes, in most cases. Dogs and cats can usually enter if they meet EU import conditions: microchip identification, rabies vaccination, and the correct veterinary documentation for your route. Requirements depend on origin country and risk status—confirm with official sources and your vet before booking.

faq.q: Do pets need a microchip and rabies vaccination?
faq.a: In most cases, yes. Microchip identification is standard for EU pet travel, and rabies vaccination is a core requirement. There is often a waiting period after vaccination before travel is allowed. The sequence and timing depend on your route—confirm current rules.

faq.q: Can pets travel in the airplane cabin?
faq.a: Sometimes. It depends on the airline, route, and pet size. Some carriers allow small dogs or cats in the cabin in an approved carrier; others only accept pets in the hold or as cargo. Confirm with the airline before booking.

faq.q: Is quarantine required for pets entering the Netherlands?
faq.a: For many routes, no—pets that meet EU requirements often do not face quarantine. Rules depend on origin country and risk status. Always verify official requirements for your situation.

faq.q: Are pets allowed in Dutch rental housing?
faq.a: It depends on the landlord and building. Some rentals allow pets; others prohibit them or set conditions. Confirm the pet policy before signing a lease. Pet-friendly homes can take longer to find in competitive markets.
